text: The surgical team works on dissection along the inferior border of the pancreas using robotic instruments. A small venous tear occurs, initially attempted to be controlled with bipolar energy and a vessel sealer, but both methods prove ineffective. Repeated attempts with electrocautery fail to control the hemorrhage, highlighting the need for adequate exposure and alternative methods.

The surgeon exchanges the vessel sealer for a needle driver and loads a 4-0 proline suture with a lapar tie into the abdomen. A simple figure of eight suture is demonstrated to control the bleeding, with the needle held in the left hand to maintain tension on the suture.

During the robotic Whipple procedure, the surgeon encounters bleeding at the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) stump on the specimen side. Bipolar electrocautery is initially used but exacerbates the bleeding. The surgeon applies pressure with a grasper and deploys a rescue stitch, placing a figure of eight suture to control the arterial bleeding. Gentle traction is maintained on the suture while performing additional throws to secure hemostasis. The pancreatic parenchyma is used to stabilize the needle, and a laparotide is placed to secure the suture. Due to the challenging dissection and potential for further bleeding, the procedure is converted to open surgery.

We'd like to emphasize at this point that there are a number of options once you actually have control of bleeding. In this case, we did opt to convert due to anticipated further difficult dissection, but we have successfully used the rescue stitch to avoid conversion in cases where we have had minor venous bleeding up to bleeding involving the vena cava with multiple binotomies and successful repair with the rescue stitch and have avoided converting to open. We hope you found this video useful. As a final recommendation, we do recommend that you practice this in simulation with your robotic team as we feel that rehearsal and preparation are some of the best tools that you can have in your arsenal to prepare for bleeding encountered in complex robotic surgery. Thank you again for your time and thank you for watching.
